key_insight: A contact-resolved finite-element model shows that Contour Neurovascular System deployment is path-dependent, with final implant shape strongly governed by wire-wire and wire-wall contact mechanics.
engineering_relevance: Accurately simulating aneurysm implant deployment can improve predictions of neck coverage, wall apposition, and migration resistance for patient-specific treatment planning.
method: Model the CNS as a dual-layer interwoven Nitinol braid using geometrically exact beam elements.
Represent the aneurysm vessel wall as a deformable hyperelastic shell.
Apply non-linear frictional contact with staged release to capture deployment, sliding, and anchoring.
evidence_signal: The framework was evaluated on three anatomical phenotypes, showing strong sensitivity of equilibrium morphology to tangential slip resistance and release depth.
watch_item: Results may depend strongly on friction/contact parameter calibration, so near-stick or frictionless assumptions can misrepresent real deployment behavior.
paper_title: Contact-resolved deployment of the Contour Neurovascular System in patient-specific intracranial aneurysms
